This checks are needed due to the way polish PESEL identification number is created. Here a bit of explanation from (Wiki)[https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/PESEL]:
>The PESEL system has been designed to cover five centuries. To distinguish people born in     different centuries, numbers are added to the MM field: 
>  * for birthdates between 1900 and 1999 - no change to MM field is made (see below)
>  * for other birthdates:
>      * 1800-1899 - month field number is increased by 80
>       * 2000-2099 - month + 20
>      * 2100-2199 - month + 40
>       * 2200-2299 - month + 60
>For example, a person born on December 24, 2002 would have a PESEL number starting with 023224 and person born on December 24, 1902 would have a PESEL number starting with 021224.

It depends - let me explain why I removed the default values from the UtilityFunction class:

As far as I can tell instances of the UtilityFunction class are normally only created internally by the BayesianOptimization class - not by the user himself. The user only uses the maximize method (which still has default values).

The relevant line of code in method maximize is:

```
def maximize(self, init_points=5, n_iter=25, acq='ucb', kappa=2.576, xi=0.0, test_random_points=False, **gp_params):
	...
	self.util = UtilityFunction(kind=acq, kappa=kappa, xi=xi)
```
	
Here all parameters (acq, kappa, xi,...) are known and all parameters have to be passed on to the UtilityFunction class anyway. Forgetting to specify one of the parameters at that point should actually result in an error.

So I'd remove the default values from the UtilityFunction class, unless there are use cases where the user wants to create UtilityFunction instances himself.


Another (and design technically better) solution would be to split the UtilityFunction class into multiple classes for UCB, EI and POI.. then UCB gets a kappa parameter and EI and POI get a xi parameter.. but I wouldn't do that yet / until more acquisition functions with even more different parameters get added.

Yes, for example, `aModelPath` is checked in the constructor. If there is a problem, an error message is printed and an invalid `Model` is constructed.

So _programmatically_ there's no way, other than grepping the error logs, for a client of this API to deal with such errors.

For example, say if Deep Speech were being used in a Scout, Mozilla newest hypothetical voice assistant, which has no screen only a voice based UI. Somehow during a model update, say, the path is set incorrectly. How does Scout know this? Grepping the error log? This seems like the wrong thing to force on Scout.

Scout should be able to catch C++ exceptions, likely not the right solution as, for example, RAII might no be consistently used, or check for error codes, much easier and lower risk, or do something else. But forcing a program to grep the error log seems like the wrong thing to do. 

So in summary I am saying we must deal with error conditions in the API or the API can't really be used in production systems that have to handle errors gracefully.

Isn't entropy as Second moment just as confusing?
Having mode and entropy for nominals is great, but the labels will be more intuitive (i.e. fewer StackExchange duplicates) if set to Mean and Std (or Center and Dispersion, or Mean/Mode and Std/Entropy). You can also tutor the user with `OWWidget.UserAdviceMessages` and header item tooltips. But I'll wager far more people understand the concepts mean, mode, std and (possibly) entropy than first and second moment. I'm normally strongly opposed to dumbing stuff down for idiots, but this really is making it complicated for no good reason. CC: @biolab/orange 

Min and Max _do_ make sense for datetime variables. For nominals, you could show the extreme labels from `Variable.values`. This is sensible when nominal `variable.ordered` (e.g. `few < more < many`). The widget is not too wide to add two more columns.

Re quartiles, they are not as essential as they can be (roughly) visually inferred from the histogram and the aforementioned reference points. 😄

Actually, *is* there a way other than using ``get`` to create a transaction? It seems to me that the transaction manager is always involved in starting transactions. But what it may not be involved in is work done outside the actual transaction boundaries. 

Perhaps there's a terminology problem here. There's an actual Transaction object, and the transaction manager is always aware of when that begins (because it creates it). But there are people that do work before a Transaction has begun, and would like that work to be reflected in an implicitly begun Transaction; they think of all the work they do as a logical transaction (little t). This would be fine, except there may be synchronizers that do "destructive" things when a Transaction is begun (ZODB polling being a prominent example).

The short answer is "you're doing it wrong" if you expect that to work, IMHO. This is a logical inconsistency in the API and the way that Transactions work. 

Can we deprecate the implicit-begin behaviour of get(), such that sometime in the future it either returns None if no transaction has begun (which will make debugging the issue easier, callers will get exceptions immediately if they assume a transaction can just be started) or calls newTransaction if there is no transaction (potentially less invasive, but also potentially harder to debug)? This is probably the wrong place to discuss this...

```python
while True:
  n = next_frame_size(socket)
  if n == 0:
    break
  while n > 0:
    result = read(socket, n)
    n -= len(result)
    yield result
```

> Should maybe this function not use the repeated capability? It seems like a different use.

I definitely don't think it works as it is in the PR right now:

``` python
send_software_edge_trigger(trigger)
```

Valid use cases would be:

``` python
session.script_triggers[1].send_software_edge_trigger(nifgen.Trigger.SCRIPT)
session["None"].send_software_edge_trigger(nifgen.Trigger.START)
```

If you use the `script_triggers container` then trigger **must be** `nifgen.Trigger.SCRIPT `. That's silly and redundant.

User has no good way of knowing `session.script_triggers["None"]` is what myst be passed if you want to send a start software trigger.

If we didn't make `viConstString triggerID` a repeated capability:

``` python
session.send_software_edge_trigger(nifgen.Trigger.SCRIPT, "ScriptTrigger1")
session.send_software_edge_trigger(nifgen.Trigger.START, "None")
```

User would need to know the magic strings. This is weird but we can live with it.

Ideally, arguably, usage should be:

``` python
session.script_triggers[1].send_software_edge_trigger()
session.send_software_edge_trigger() # sends start trigger
```

No enum needed. I'm not sure this is the best way to go, and I think it would require at the very least a fancy wrapper.

There is no filesystem knowledge in the current REST API. The Contents API path is exposed, but that need not map to any filesystem at all. The SessionManager asks the ContentsManager "What should the CWD of the kernel be for this file/notebook". The *client* of the session API does not have any way to influence this, and it shouldn't for notebooks. For notebooks, the information should continue to be just two pieces of information: the notebook path according to the ContentsManager and the kernel name, according to the KernelManager.

def migrate(session):

from cassandra_migrate.migration import get_current_session

The configuration directives inside blocks apply globally, so it's not that big of an issue. However people be overwriting some values in nested blocks containing VirtualHosts. Then again people could similarly have things like:
```
Listen 80
<VirtualHost *:80>
...
</VirtualHost>
```
as an example, which would result to apache failing to address already in use error. So it kind of goes both ways.

I think we should just copy the VirtualHost itself. Linux distributions provide examples for, and encourage people to use a structure where VirtualHosts are sitting on root level of the configuration file. The IfModules and IfDefines are pretty much just in place to prevent loading of certain (default) config blocks if modules are not enabled. 

Also I doubt people play around with the modules a lot. Basically, if you have your configuration running, you do have the modules in anyway, and the point where it would break (in configtest syntax check fail way of breaking) would be when you would disable a module manually later on.

If anything, I would say we'd need to put more effort into active module parsing and not peeking in to configuration structures under disabled IfModule statements, hence not offering the disabled domains as installation candidates by Certbot.

In a world where we wouldn't need to revisit the configuration ever again, or give user a clear indication of the new vhost file we created - we'd create the new VirtualHost in the original file, right beside the original one, hence leveraging all the nested config directives in place.

I don't have a Yandex account to test this, but based on inspection of the Lexicon provider, I don't _think_ you want this here.

If you have a domain registered with Yandex (e.g., example.com) and you try to issue a certificate for a nested sub-domain (e.g., foo.bar.baz.example.com), what happens?

(Usually, the `_find_domain_id` method which calls this usually relies on "No domain found" to realize that it needs to check bar.baz.example.com, baz.example.com, and then example.com — which is what the DNS provider recognizes. This check would interrupt that functionality and treat the "No domain found" as an error to report instead of ignoring it while we look for a domain the provider recognizes. It's possible that the Yandex API behaves a bit differently than others here, but the Lexicon provider doesn't convey that.)

The mock layer works such that its `FakedSession` is instantiated by the testcase using the mock layer instead of the real `Session`. For example, the find() function on CPCs is still available in the same way it would be with a real `Session`, but when going through the `FakedSession`, it becomes a `list()` on the `FakedCpcManager` of the `FakedHmc`. The bottom line is, the methods of the `FakedHmc` class are low level add/remove/list methods that are used internally by the mock layer, and therefore it is not meant to be used directly and therefore not compatible with the external API.
